Front: When did James I become king of England?
Back: 1603 after the death of Elizabeth I

Front: What union occurred in 1603?
Back: The Union of the Crowns between England and Scotland

Front: What was James I's belief in kingship?
Back: The Divine Right of Kings

Front: What did the Divine Right of Kings mean?
Back: That monarchs were chosen by God and not accountable to Parliament

Front: What was the Hampton Court Conference?
Back: A meeting in 1604 between James I and religious leaders

Front: What was produced after the Hampton Court Conference?
Back: The King James Bible in 1611

Front: Who were the Puritans?
Back: Protestants who wanted further reform of the Church of England

Front: What was the Gunpowder Plot?
Back: A Catholic conspiracy to blow up Parliament in 1605

Front: Who led the Gunpowder Plot?
Back: Robert Catesby

Front: Who was Guy Fawkes?
Back: A conspirator caught guarding gunpowder under Parliament

Front: Why did the Gunpowder Plot fail?
Back: Authorities discovered the conspiracy before it could be carried out

Front: Why did tensions exist between James I and Parliament?
Back: Disagreements over money and royal authority

Front: What financial problem faced James I?
Back: Royal debts and insufficient income

Front: What were impositions?
Back: Customs duties introduced without Parliamentary approval

Front: Why were impositions controversial?
Back: They bypassed Parliament's control over taxation

Front: When did Charles I become king?
Why did Charles I clash with Parliament?
Back: He believed strongly in royal authority

Front: What was the Petition of Right?
Back: A Parliamentary document limiting royal power in 1628

Front: Why did Charles I accept the Petition of Right?
Back: He needed Parliamentary funds

Front: What was the Personal Rule?
Back: Charles I ruling without Parliament from 1629 to 1640

Front: What was ship money?
Back: A tax traditionally raised for naval defence

Front: Why was ship money controversial?
Back: Charles I imposed it during peacetime without Parliament

Front: Who challenged ship money in court?
Back: John Hampden

Front: What religious policy caused tension under Charles I?
Back: Promotion of Arminianism

Front: Who was William Laud?
Back: Archbishop of Canterbury supporting religious reforms

Front: Why did Puritans oppose Laud?
Back: They saw his reforms as too Catholic

Front: What caused the Bishops' Wars?
Back: Charles I's attempt to impose the English Prayer Book in Scotland

Front: When did the Bishops' Wars occur?
Why did Charles I call the Short Parliament?
Back: He needed money to fight Scotland

Front: Why did the Short Parliament end quickly?
Back: Parliament refused funding without addressing grievances

Front: What was the Long Parliament?
Back: A Parliament called in 1640 that lasted many years

Front: What reforms did the Long Parliament introduce?
Back: Limiting royal authority and abolishing key royal courts

Front: What was the Triennial Act?
Back: A law requiring Parliament to be called every three years

Front: What happened to Thomas Wentworth?
Back: He was executed for treason in 1641

Front: What was the Grand Remonstrance?
Back: A list of grievances against Charles I

Front: Why did the Grand Remonstrance increase tensions?
Back: It revealed deep divisions in Parliament

Front: What happened in January 1642?
Back: Charles I attempted to arrest five members of Parliament

Front: Why was this event significant?
Back: It marked the breakdown of relations with Parliament

Front: When did the English Civil War begin?
Who were the Royalists?
Back: Supporters of King Charles I

Front: Who were the Parliamentarians?
Back: Supporters of Parliament

Front: Why did many nobles support the king?
Back: They believed in traditional monarchy

Front: Why did many towns support Parliament?
Back: They feared royal absolutism

Front: What was the Battle of Edgehill?
Back: The first major battle of the Civil War in 1642

Front: Why was the Battle of Marston Moor significant?
Back: It gave Parliament control of northern England

Front: When did the Battle of Marston Moor occur?
What was the New Model Army?
Back: A professional army created by Parliament

Front: Who helped create the New Model Army?
Back: Oliver Cromwell and Sir Thomas Fairfax

Front: Why was the New Model Army effective?
Back: It was disciplined and promoted on merit

Front: What was the Battle of Naseby?
Back: A decisive Parliamentary victory in 1645

Front: Why was the Battle of Naseby important?
Back: It destroyed the main Royalist army

Front: What happened to Charles I after the war?
Back: He was captured by Parliamentary forces

Front: Why did divisions emerge within Parliament?
Back: Different views about religion and power

Front: Who were the Levellers?
Back: A political group demanding greater political equality

Front: What was Pride's Purge?
Back: The removal of MPs opposed to the trial of the king

Front: When did Pride's Purge occur?
What was the Rump Parliament?
Back: The remaining MPs after Pride's Purge

Front: When was Charles I executed?
Back: 30 January 1649

Front: Why was Charles I executed?
Back: He was found guilty of treason against the nation

Front: Why was the execution of Charles I significant?
Back: It ended the monarchy temporarily

Front: What government replaced the monarchy?
Back: The Commonwealth

Front: Who became the dominant leader of the Commonwealth?
Back: Oliver Cromwell

Front: What was the Commonwealth?
Back: A republic established after the king's execution

Front: Why did Cromwell dismiss the Rump Parliament?
Back: He believed it was ineffective

Front: When did Cromwell become Lord Protector?
What was the Protectorate?
Back: Cromwell's military government

Front: Why did Cromwell rely on the army?
Back: To maintain stability and authority

Front: What were the Major Generals?
Back: Military governors ruling regions of England

Front: Why were the Major Generals unpopular?
Back: They imposed strict moral laws

Front: When did Cromwell die?
Who succeeded Cromwell?
Back: His son Richard Cromwell

Front: Why did Richard Cromwell lose power?
Back: He lacked support from the army

Front: When was the monarchy restored?
Who became king in 1660?
Back: Charles II

Front: What was the Restoration?
Back: The return of the monarchy after the Commonwealth

Front: Why was the Restoration significant?
Back: It ended the republican experiment in England
